does: This paper measures the implicit reasoning capabilities of frontier AI models when operating without explicit chain-of-thought tokens across over 30,000 questions spanning 43 benchmarks covering mathematics, coding, puzzles, causality, theory-of-mind, and strategic reasoning. The core contribution is introducing the task-completion time horizon (TH) metric, which estimates the human time required to solve tasks that a model completes with 50% success rate, alongside a reasoning token horizon measuring the minimum o3-mini reasoning tokens needed for equivalent performance. The authors find that frontier models like GPT-5.5 achieve no-CoT completion times exceeding 3 minutes with reasoning token horizons above 1,500 tokens, and observe a doubling of no-CoT task-completion time horizons approximately yearly over six years, projecting potential horizons exceeding 7 minutes by 2028.
connects: This work responds to growing concerns about AI safety oversight that relies on monitoring explicit chain-of-thought reasoning in frontier models. It builds on earlier research showing that transparent reasoning traces enable better interpretability and oversight, and extends this by empirically testing whether models can perform complex reasoning implicitly without external visibility. The paper connects to benchmark-based evaluation methods common in the field while introducing a novel time-horizon framing that bridges human and machine reasoning capabilities. It engages with concerns raised in AI safety literature about deceptive alignment and hidden reasoning, and relates to prior work on measuring and comparing model reasoning sophistication across diverse cognitive domains.
enables: This work points toward critical questions about the future oversight of advanced AI systems if implicit reasoning capabilities continue scaling according to observed trends. It enables more rigorous evaluation of whether current safety monitoring approaches relying on CoT transparency remain viable as models advance, potentially motivating development of alternative oversight and interpretability techniques for systems performing substantial hidden reasoning. The time-horizon framework could facilitate longitudinal tracking of implicit reasoning capabilities and inform policy decisions about AI deployment safety. The findings may catalyze research into detecting and constraining unobserved reasoning, understanding the mechanisms enabling implicit computation, and designing robust oversight methods that don't depend on explicit reasoning tokens.
